topic: Machine Learning: Classification vs Regression Scenarios

questions.scenario: A financial technology company is developing an algorithmic credit risk assessment engine to estimate the 'Loss Given Default' (LGD) for enterprise loans. LGD represents the percentage of the loan amount lost if a borrower defaults. Analyzing historical data reveals a highly skewed, zero-inflated distribution: 92% of the borrowers do not default (loss is exactly 0), while the remaining 8% exhibit a continuous range of loss percentages between 5% and 100%. The data science team is debating whether to treat this as a single unified regression model or a multi-stage modeling pipeline.
questions.question: Which of the following architectural paradigms and evaluation strategies is most mathematically and practically sound for this scenario?
options: A) Train a single Deep Neural Network regression model using Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss, as its high capacity will naturally fit both the zero-spike and the continuous tail.

B) Implement a two-stage Hurdle model: first, a binary classifier to predict the probability of default, and second, a regressor (such as Gamma or Beta regression) trained exclusively on the non-zero default instances.
C) Apply a standard logarithmic transform to the target variable, train a single Ridge Regression model, and evaluate model performance strictly using the coefficient of determination (R-squared).
D) Discretize the continuous loss percentage into 100 equal-width bins, treat the task as a 100-class classification problem, and optimize using categorical cross-entropy.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) Implement a two-stage Hurdle model: first, a binary classifier to predict the probability of default, and second, a regressor (such as Gamma or Beta regression) trained exclusively on the non-zero default instances.
questions.explanation: Zero-inflated continuous data violates basic assumptions of standard regression algorithms (such as homoscedasticity and normality of residuals). A single regression model optimized via MSE will heavily bias its predictions toward zero, severely underestimating the loss in true default scenarios. A Hurdle/two-stage model mathematically decouples the decision boundary of occurrence (classification: default vs. non-default) from the intensity of the outcome (regression: how much is lost). Using a Gamma or Beta distribution for the second stage is ideal because LGD is bounded and non-negative, preventing physically impossible predictions (e.g., negative losses).

questions.scenario: An autonomous drone manufacturing company is designing an on-board, real-time obstacle avoidance classifier. The system processes high-dimensional LiDAR point cloud vectors (exceeding 120,000 features per frame) and must classify the environment into 'Safe to Proceed' or 'Immediate Hazard'. The processing unit on-board the drone is an edge computing chip with severe memory limitations and a strict hard real-time latency budget of less than 3 milliseconds per inference.
questions.question: To meet both the high-dimensionality challenge and the extreme inference latency constraint, which machine learning pipeline should the system architect select?
options: A) Apply Kernel Principal Component Analysis (KPCA) to reduce the features to 100, and classify using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel.
B) Apply Random Projection (based on the Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma) to reduce dimensionality to 200, followed by a sparse L1-regularized Logistic Regression classifier.
C) Pass the raw 120,000-dimensional features directly into a Naive Bayes classifier, leveraging its conditional independence assumption to calculate probabilities in O(d) time.
D) Train an ensemble of 800 Deep Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDT) directly on the raw feature space, utilizing on-board parallel GPU execution to guarantee speed.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) Apply Random Projection (based on the Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma) to reduce dimensionality to 200, followed by a sparse L1-regularized Logistic Regression classifier.
questions.explanation: To meet a <3ms latency budget on resource-constrained edge hardware, computational complexity must be minimized. Kernel PCA and RBF-SVM scale poorly at inference time because they require calculating kernel distances against a large set of support vectors, violating real-time constraints. Ensembles of 800 GBDTs are too heavy for memory and edge processors. Random projection is extremely fast because it projects the high-dimensional space into a lower dimension using a random matrix, preserving pairwise distances without expensive eigen-decompositions. Pair this with a sparse L1-regularized Logistic Regression, and the inference step is simplified to a highly optimized, sparse vector dot product, completing in microseconds.

questions.scenario: An enterprise SaaS platform wants to predict whether high-value corporate clients will churn (cancel their subscription). The dataset is highly imbalanced, with a churn rate of only 0.15% (highly skewed). The business operations team establishes that a False Negative (failing to identify a client who will churn) is 40 times more costly to the company than a False Positive (incorrectly flagging a loyal client as a risk, which triggers a simple automated check-in email).
questions.question: How should the development team design the optimization objective and evaluation framework to align with these business realities?
options: A) Train a standard Random Forest classifier to optimize Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) curve, setting the classification threshold at 0.5.
B) Reformulate the task as a regression problem to predict the exact remaining contract days, optimize using Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and flag any client with less than 30 days left.
C) Apply Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) to achieve a perfect 50:50 class balance, and optimize the model using raw Classification Accuracy.
D) Train a classifier using a cost-sensitive loss function (assigning a 40x penalty to False Negatives) and optimize the model using the F-beta score metric with beta set to 2.
questions.correct_answer_text: D) Train a classifier using a cost-sensitive loss function (assigning a 40x penalty to False Negatives) and optimize the model using the F-beta score metric with beta set to 2.
questions.explanation: When dealing with highly imbalanced data combined with asymmetric business costs, standard metrics and default loss functions fail. Maximizing AUROC or raw accuracy ignores the uneven cost distribution, as a model can achieve 99.85% accuracy by simply predicting 'no churn' for everyone. Incorporating cost-sensitive learning directly into the loss function (by weighting the binary cross-entropy) forces the decision boundary to skew in favor of catching the minority class (reducing False Negatives). Evaluating using the F-beta metric (where beta = 2) mathematically weights Recall higher than Precision, aligning directly with the business's high cost for missed detections.
